speaker: interviewer
text: Wemurth, and this is tape number nine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have stepped out a life out of the gas chambers. It came later to me that I was stood in the place probably of the biggest death cry ever heard, where so many thousands in crammed condition were put in. And when the big trains arrived, not our small group, where they have pushed in people to capacity, and in order for production to go faster and better, thrown the little ones on top of them. I stood at that spot. But these thoughts only came later to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father in the same barrack as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were always together. When we had-- it was at a later stage when we parted. But we'd come to that later-- always together. Well, it was part of our survival, of course. Anyway, we stood in front of the designated barracks for hours in hot sunshine. We were alive, but at the same time after a while, you also get tired. And you get hungry. Eventually, a kapo came, which the so-called [NON-ENGLISH] in this particular camp. He was dressed in the same pajama pants, striped suits, but his trousers were ironed. His jacket was made to measure. And his hat even had a sort of, like a point, as if to imitate the military hat. But it had no shield, of course. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he told us that we shouldn't expect anything for three days. Newcomers don't get anything for three days. And he wants to impress us, and he did duly by telling us that to make room for us, the night before they killed 4,000 gypsies. Of course, we were duly impressed. We also found out that these gypsies, before they were killed, were given the night before a good meal, sausages, and what have you. But something, of course, which don't get in Auschwitz. And for the next eight days, I was in this particular camp. If you call is a separate eight days, I call it all Auschwitz, also the next one. And that would have been one more in the count, but it doesn't really matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you one thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put a number on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is going to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. They hadn't done that yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: You anticipate, yeah. OK, now, it was another couple came to him, and then we were let in. It was like old wooden boards. You know, you can't call them beds-- [INAUDIBLE], I'm not sure what you call them. And we were glad to rest on those.

speaker: interviewer
text: As many as they were, we had to stay in close proximity with everybody else. And my father, of course, next to me. The next day, we were called out and give the number. And was-- was it a bit of cowardice? I let my father go first tattooing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got the number [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] B3406. And I have 3B407. Now, in Auschwitz terms, actually, the Hungarians got mostly the B numbers, because they had run out of Polish death candidates already to a great extent. The Polish ghettos were already mostly liquidated at that stage. And at that time, the transport arrived from Hungary. Anyway, we had the tattoos, but no work yet. After about a week-- while at the evening, we were given, by the way, we were given the first evening some watery soup. Well, better than nothing, if you're being told that you get nothing for three days. And later on, I will tell you about the bread ration. But we come to that when I, sort of, more in sequence with the book. After eight days, we were called. And somebody asked professions. Now, something that I wished I hadn't done-- twice, it was bad. But this time, it was not so bad. I took my file under the arm. We went forward. And we both said we were painters. And they rejected us. They had too many painters, or he didn't like us. I'm not sure. We went back. But whatever happens, I said, we must get into that group because we have heard enough stories by that time within eight days, what happened to people. And the way to survive us, I mean, in our group we initially all survived. It was a young strong group, good slave material. And when other people in other groups arrived, then those young and strong ones survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were told by the other inmates. Can you see the smoke there? Yes. These are your parents, your brothers, sisters, what have you. Now, we knew how dangerous the place was. And we were trying-- perhaps, you know. But to be classed as non-skilled, we were afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure if I knew already that we were going to be sent into another camp from there. Anyway I was determined to join that group. And there was a lot of things that we were called-- they were calling for [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] are carpenters or joiners. Now, we spread out. My father came from the left. I came from the right. And not many of them were, [LAUGHS] sort of saying that they knew about this profession. And we were accepted. We were going to be-- to worry later what's going to happen, you know. Then, I don't know if it was the same day or the next day, we were going to be marched out into what turned out to be the nearby camp of Buna, Monowitz. It is only-- I'm not sure-- 10 kilometers away, a few kilometers. I'm not sure how far. However, when we stood in line there to march out, I suddenly heard something incredible. Music, beautiful music, Viennese music-- and at the same time, we were inspected like by a group of SS in uniform. And I don't know whether it was the commandant again. One of them, again, the tallest, stepped forward. He was about 15, 20 yards away from me. Called out a prisoner. I don't know what he said. You know, something wrong with him, had to adjust something. But he had to be punished. Now, in order-- he did all that, clearly, to show his party trick. His party trick was to step on the toes of the prisoner. And with his hand, and with a jerk, pushed his forehead. So not being able to step backward, he fell backwards. And then he let go the legs, if-- you know-- and I would say, the harder was the fall. And he stayed down properly for good. I'm sure for good, because that was a very loud fall. We could hear it. And we were marching out. And the music changing to a march, loverly march music. And I was thinking, this guy, this boy on the floor, probably will never hear music again. These were my last thoughts. But at the same time, I also found this beautiful music to be a mockery. And we later heard about the musicians of Auschwitz. I'm sure you did. Well, the SS took the biggest talent and had a-- made themselves a nice life. Dances with music, and had plenty of talents to choose from. Probably if they had enough musicians, they sort of-- music, we don't need you. Go in the gas chambers. But those who were-- did get a job, apparently survived. A lot of them, anyway, because there were films about the musicians of Auschwitz. And I actually met one in Frankfurt. But that's another story. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know I keep it as this story, once I've mentioned it. I was given a medal for resisting Nazi dictatorship in Frankfurt last September. And one of them was this musician as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it at that time that I met him? It doesn't really matter. That's in-- as I say, I was in Frankfurt. And I met this man. But he came from California. [? Meier, ?] I think his name was called. Just while you've mentioned the medal, in case we don't go back to it, who-- I know it was awarded to you by the Germans. But who honored you with that? Did somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were 17 people of us who were honored that were in the resistance. Only two Jewish people, and I was one of the two. The others were. Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you in the resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in the resistence. It was for trying to kill Hitler. And that was the resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they know that you had tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably, they checked my stories. Checked the papers. Checked that it was the time when Hitler went to the Eastern Front, and [LAUGHS] well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: So who told them that you had done the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw of it-- my book was read by a German and by a Frankfurter paper. And so you know, the ball started rolling from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't because somebody had witnessed you doing this, and were going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, somebody read my book, and a German paper sort of criss-cross checked it, and, you know, then printed it. And so the ball started rolling from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I show the medal-- medal later. Well, where were we now?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's quite often that I'll ask that question. But hey, if you don't remember, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I do remember you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah yes, and we marched out. And it was nearby that we reached the Buna works, or Monowitz, or whatever the name was. And it was actually a-- under work done by the IG Farben of Hoechst, which is near Frankfurt, and still exists.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you have called the march a death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was not a death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you have-- how far was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was only a few kilometers. And I was still strong from a previous camp. No, no. Anyway, at this particular camp, at first we were put into a big tent. And we had to work, like beginners all had to work, on building streets or whatever have you. You know, hard work. We came home in the evening, and there was already a queue. And we had a bowl of soup. And I was eating it slowly, but I shouldn't have because others were eating it quickly. And I noticed why. Because the queue formed again, and whatever was left, was dished out again. And I tried to, because I didn't have enough, to go into the queue. But when I was nearly there, there was nothing left. And I remember this was the one and only time I actually went into my-- there was a [INAUDIBLE] type of-- not a public bed. I'm not sure what it was. And lied-- lied-- laid down there, and cried with hunger. But very soon I stopped, and got a very strong determination that this has to change. From then on, we were both looking every day after work, going around looking, hoping, to find friends or somebody we know who could help us either with work, or with food, or with something. And as weeks went by, we were given other-- we were given proper barracks afterwards. As weeks went by, we had hard work and not enough food. We could see that we were losing strength, losing weight. We also heard for the first time the word 'muselmann.' Now, muselmann actually got a new meaning there. The actual word of muselmann means like a-- a-- like a-- Mohammedan priest, sort of priest. And there are some who, because of the fanaticism, sort of don't eat and get very thin. I'm not quite sure. Somebody called that word, and it stuck. Like for instance, Kanada Kommand, the people who worked-- worked, received a transport, and had everything. People brought along food. You name it, they had it. And it was probably Canada in the thought that it's a country where everything is still available. I'm not sure. Somebody coined it, and so it was called Kanada Kommand, Kommando. And we saw for the first time these so-called muselmann were skeletal figures, very thin already, and on the way out, almost too far gone to ever recover. Some of them even if they did get food, they wouldn't. And in most cases, these people either dropped. Or if there was a selection, they would have been taken away to a place of recuperation-- the gas chambers. Now, I said to my father, if we don't have any change in our situation, we will soon be one of them. And then my father, luckily, met somebody who he knew from Frankfurt. Name is Tiba. And he was in the administration writing. And we said, we came here actually as carpenters. We should have asked for something else, but soon we got a job as so-called carpenters. And I was afraid what the Germans would think, because I never could hit a nail straight. But I was sort of like my father. He was-- he knew everything. He could do everything, being practical to excess almost. And anyway, my job was nearby they had started a so-called cooling tower. Whatever the function of that is, I haven't learned, because I think it was bombed later. With the only bomb that fell in Auschwitz, but I come to that later. I was already about-- given work about six or eight meters high. And my father worked from the ground. But my work was to-- boards had been passed to me. Though it was a-- a metal and steel skeleton of a tower we had surrounded with wooden planks. And my job was to get it from lower down and pass it to the person who nailed a screw in it to the next, you know. And so we had to work on, bit by bit. Now, where I was working on, was about 5 centimeters, about two inches wide. Now, I could hold myself, because this tower was built that from one end to the other end, there were besides the level ones, there were on zig up, and zag going down, either girders. And the more I went to the middle, my hand was higher. And I had to bend down at the same time. But the fellow next to me, he could at least hold himself on the previous board.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- then I remember the balancing act. Because when it was too high, for the next bow that came down low, and I could hold myself, was maybe a couple of meters, or six feet or so, where I had to-- I had to balance up high there. And I could see it's going too high about five, six, or seven high, floors high. I was dreading it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to go to the next. And I had to balance with outstretched arms on this. It wasn't exactly a rope, but it was no more than about two inches wide, and without holding myself. And of course, one little step wrong, and I'm done. I was glad to reach the next one. Anyway, I already sort of got used to that. And then in the end, we were already one higher still another. And I didn't say I looked forward to that little bit from zig to zag, but I was thinking, after the war, I'm going to join the high life, a-- I said, as a-- rope--

speaker: interviewer
text: High wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: A rope high wire walk, yeah. Anyway, one day, a [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, but you were asking to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be replaced. No, we didn't want a job anymore. We asked my father, please, ask the men for another job. And well, one evening, I found a classmate. And he was a kapo. But whereas most kapos were scoundrels, criminals, there were others who were not. And he was one of the few. And how he got to become a kapo, I don't know. He spoke German, wasn't very tall. He was a classmate, Adolf Konig. And he-- we joined his commando. And he always-- you know, it was, at the moment, at first, it was also in a place where I did-- had to do some drawings. Later on, it was also on the street. But not very, very hard work, he saw to it. Now, I'm not quite sure in my story where-- when it happened, but a little interlude where we went out on street, where it could have been very much in the beginning actually, as you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: As I told you, I switch sometimes back. Well, the kapo who was with us-- no, it was a previous kapo it wasn't this. It was before Adolf Konig. He liked to disappear. And he asked, was there ever anybody a foreman amongst you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, that's an opportunity. I pointed at my father, who marched a couple of rows before me. And I said in a very sort of crude German, that one there. He was. Pointed at my father. He was immediately sort of elevated. But of course, he did not shout or hit out. But he watched, rather. If nobody came, we didn't have to work hard. But if somebody did come, we were grateful. Everybody was. We worked harder then.

speaker: interviewer
text: This little interlude aside, I ask Adolf Konig. Now, we are very hungry. Do you know how we can get some more food? He said, why didn't you ask me before? There was another classmate who worked in the kitchen. Now, six weeks in Auschwitz, and we had easy jobs and enough to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not quite-- at first, we had to sacrifice half a day's bread ration to get what I call [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], an elliptical sort of container which contained one liter of food. But when I got this food, he took it from the bottom. And it was thick stuff. And it helped us, but it wasn't enough. Now, having mentioned a half a ration of bread, I better tell you what a ration was. A ration was, if you know today's small [INAUDIBLE], cut it in four. It will be for four people. Now, when it was cut, it was eyed with eagle eyes, because everybody wanted to have the bit that looked a little bit longer as if his life depended on it. And probably, it did. Most-- many people ate it at once. Maybe cut it in two. My father even managed to cut it-- we had sort of a spoon sharpened like a knife, the edge of a spoon, cut it even four small slices to go during the day. I don't know if it helps. I don't know which is the better system. But that was the bread ration, and sometimes, a bit of margarine, which is the size of a sugar cube or two. And black so-called coffee-- I'm not sure what it was. But real coffee it wasn't. But at one time, I thought I had diarrhea. And I thought, well-- anyway, at the sediments of the coffee, I sort of took it out with my fingers. And I added, and it filled the stomach a bit. And also, by whatever reason, it cured my diarrhea. Anyway, soon, it was-- at one time, as my thoughts go, sometimes I drop myself and speak. You know, go along with our thoughts. At one time, my other classmate, Max Spira, whom I managed to locate in America and rang recently to say another thank you, out of [INAUDIBLE] I met again once in Frankfurt after the war. But never again, nobody knows about him. Anyway, he asked me if I wanted diet food. I said, sounds good. Give me diet food. And it was semolina, sweet. It tasted good, but it didn't really fill us. And I never asked for it again. But can you imagine in Auschwitz, a camp where a person doesn't count anything, it's like the music. Semolina, there's something I can't even explain. It sounds ridiculous. It is ridiculous. And I can't explain it. But we didn't ask for it again. Then with a bigger sacrifice, we got ourselves a bigger [NON-ENGLISH], one that contained three liters of soup. Now, in order to do that, Max Spira was afraid sort of. Could lose his job or worse. He said, you do something for me. I get up-- I had to get up five minutes early in the morning and look around if there were any food containers which were taken to the barracks each day, each night, left somewhere, and bring them back. Well, it was obviously a job that wasn't needed, because in three weeks, I actually found one. It was somehow-- I don't know how it was forgotten. But it was a job. But from then on, we really got stronger again and filled out again. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a neighbor of ours, we were eating in such a way that not many people could see us, because envy. And then a neighbor did see us, and he offered us a half a portion of bread for a smaller [NON-ENGLISH]. And for him, the contents-- it was the bottom of the container was worth much more. I should have had a conscience to take his half portion of bread, but I didn't, because it was no time for having a conscience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a bit more bread. And really, we did not suffer. We had an easy job after the six weeks, and we got our strength back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, winter came. And it was cold, very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the end of '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: End of '44. Now, there comes another episode, which probably will have to go into the next reel. My father once took me behind the barracks, some barracks, and according-- as I say, practical, forever practical-- he has shown me a new underwear. They were turned inside out turned cement sacks with the necessary holes, which we then put on as underwear. [AUDIO OUT]
